candidate.foreshadow_text: Now, there were two possessions of the James Dillingham Youngs in which they both took a mighty pride. One was Jim’s gold watch that had been his father’s and his grandfather’s. The other was Della’s hair. Had the queen of Sheba lived in the flat across the airshaft, Della would have let her hair hang out the window some day to dry just to depreciate Her Majesty’s jewels and gifts. Had King Solomon been the janitor, with all his treasures piled up in the basement, Jim would have pulled out his watch every time he passed, just to see him pluck at his beard from envy.
candidate.payoff_text:
For there lay The Combs—the set of combs, side and back, that Della had worshipped long in a Broadway window. Beautiful combs, pure tortoise shell, with jewelled rims—just the shade to wear in the beautiful vanished hair. They were expensive combs, she knew, and her heart had simply craved and yearned over them without the least hope of possession. And now, they were hers, but the tresses that should have adorned the coveted adornments were gone.

But she hugged them to her bosom, and at length she was able to look up with dim eyes and a smile and say: “My hair grows so fast, Jim!”

And then Della leaped up like a little singed cat and cried, “Oh, oh!”

Jim had not yet seen his beautiful present. She held it out to him eagerly upon her open palm. The dull precious metal seemed to flash with a reflection of her bright and ardent spirit.

“Isn’t it a dandy, Jim? I hunted all over town to find it. You’ll have to look at the time a hundred times a day now. Give me your watch. I want to see how it looks on it.”

Instead of obeying, Jim tumbled down on the couch and put his hands under the back of his head and smiled.

“Dell,” said he, “let’s put our Christmas presents away and keep ’em a while. They’re too nice to use just at present. I sold the watch to get the money to buy your combs. And now suppose you put the chops on.”
candidate.foreshadow_summary: The couple's two prized possessions are introduced: Jim's gold watch (family heirloom) and Della's beautiful long hair.
candidate.payoff_summary: Della buys a platinum chain for Jim's watch, but Jim has sold the watch to buy tortoise-shell combs for Della's hair; both gifts are rendered useless.

proposed_trigger.description: Della sells her hair to Madame Sofronie for twenty dollars.
observable_conditions: P0018: Della says 'Give it to me quick' after accepting the offer.

candidate.relation_description: The early establishment of the two treasures sets up the ironic double sacrifice: Della sells her hair to buy a chain for the watch, while Jim sells the watch to buy combs for the hair, making both gifts pointless.
candidate.stage1_rationale: Clear delayed setup: prized objects introduced in P0010 are directly involved in the climactic ironic reversal in P0039-P0045. Non-trivial gap with a clear actionable trigger.

final_trigger.description: Della sells her hair to Madame Sofronie for twenty dollars.
observable_conditions: P0018: Della says 'Give it to me quick' after accepting the offer.

verdict.foreshadow_description: P0010 establishes the two prized possessions: Jim's gold watch and Della's hair, setting up the potential for sacrifice.
verdict.payoff_description: P0039-P0041 show Della receiving the combs but having no hair to wear them, and she gives Jim the watch chain, but Jim's sale of the watch is not revealed within the provided payoff window.
verdict.rationale: The payoff window (P0037-P0041) does not include the revelation that Jim sold his watch to buy the combs. Without this, the foreshadowing from P0010 regarding the watch is not resolved, making the payoff incomplete and unsupported by the given evidence.
verdict.rejection_reason: The provided payoff window does not contain the necessary information to resolve the foreshadowing: Jim's sale of the watch is missing, so the connection cannot be verified.

candidate.foreshadow_text: Out of his trance Jim seemed quickly to wake. He enfolded his Della. For ten seconds let us regard with discreet scrutiny some inconsequential object in the other direction. Eight dollars a week or a million a year—what is the difference? A mathematician or a wit would give you the wrong answer. The magi brought valuable gifts, but that was not among them. This dark assertion will be illuminated later on.
candidate.payoff_text: The magi, as you know, were wise men—wonderfully wise men—who brought gifts to the Babe in the manger. They invented the art of giving Christmas presents. Being wise, their gifts were no doubt wise ones, possibly bearing the privilege of exchange in case of duplication. And here I have lamely related to you the uneventful chronicle of two foolish children in a flat who most unwisely sacrificed for each other the greatest treasures of their house. But in a last word to the wise of these days let it be said that of all who give gifts these two were the wisest. Of all who give and receive gifts, such as they are wisest. Everywhere they are wisest. They are the magi.
candidate.foreshadow_summary: Narrator makes a cryptic remark: 'The magi brought valuable gifts, but that was not among them. This dark assertion will be illuminated later on.'
candidate.payoff_summary: The narrator reveals the moral: the young couple are the wisest gift-givers, like the magi, because they gave the greatest treasures of their house out of love.

proposed_trigger.description: Both gifts are revealed to be useless, prompting the narrator's commentary.
observable_conditions: P0045: Jim says 'I sold the watch to get the money to buy your combs.'

candidate.relation_description: The narrator's early vague statement about the magi promises a later explanation, which is delivered in the final paragraph as a thematic payoff that reframes the story's events.
candidate.stage1_rationale: Explicit narrative foreshadowing ('This dark assertion will be illuminated later on') with a clear retrospective payoff in the closing moral. Non-trivial gap and distinct trigger.

final_trigger.description: Both gifts are revealed to be useless, prompting the narrator's commentary.
observable_conditions: P0045: Jim says 'I sold the watch to get the money to buy your combs.'

verdict.foreshadow_description: The narrator mentions the magi and their valuable gifts but notes 'that was not among them', calling it a dark assertion to be illuminated later.
verdict.payoff_description: The narrator explains that the magi were wise gift-givers, and concludes that Della and Jim, who foolishly sacrificed their treasures, are actually the wisest, like the magi.
verdict.rationale: F directly introduces an unresolved claim about the magi and promises later illumination. P resolves this by redefining the couple as the true magi, completing the narrative circle. The trigger of the gifts being useless (P0045) prompts the payoff. This is a clear, non-thematic narrative recall.

candidate.foreshadow_text: Jim was never late. Della doubled the fob chain in her hand and sat on the corner of the table near the door that he always entered. Then she heard his step on the stair away down on the first flight, and she turned white for just a moment. She had a habit of saying a little silent prayer about the simplest everyday things, and now she whispered: “Please God, make him think I am still pretty.”
candidate.payoff_text: “Don’t make any mistake, Dell,” he said, “about me. I don’t think there’s anything in the way of a haircut or a shave or a shampoo that could make me like my girl any less. But if you’ll unwrap that package you may see why you had me going a while at first.”
candidate.foreshadow_summary: Della prays silently: 'Please God, make him think I am still pretty,' fearing Jim's reaction to her shorn hair.
candidate.payoff_summary: Jim explains that his shocked expression was not because she looks unattractive, but because he bought her the combs that now have no hair to adorn, saying nothing could make him like her less.

proposed_trigger.description: Jim enters and stares at Della with an unreadable, terrifying expression.
observable_conditions: P0027: 'His eyes were fixed upon Della, and there was an expression in them that she could not read, and it terrified her.'

candidate.relation_description: Della's worry about her appearance creates a misdirection: the reader and Della initially interpret Jim's stare as displeasure, but the true reason (the useless combs) is later revealed, subverting expectations.
candidate.stage1_rationale: Classic misdirection: a character's fear is set up, then resolved with an unexpected but logical explanation. The gap and trigger are clearly defined in the text.

final_trigger.description: Jim enters and stares at Della with an unreadable, terrifying expression.
observable_conditions: P0027: 'His eyes were fixed upon Della, and there was an expression in them that she could not read, and it terrified her.'

verdict.foreshadow_description: Della's silent prayer that Jim still finds her pretty establishes her fear that her haircut may have diminished his affection, creating an unresolved anxiety.
verdict.payoff_description: Jim reveals that his strange stare was not displeasure but shock that the combs he bought for her hair are now useless, recontextualizing her fear.
verdict.rationale: The setup introduces Della's concern about her appearance and Jim's potential reaction. The payoff resolves this by explaining Jim's actual feelings and the misdirection caused by the gifts. The trigger (Jim's unreadable stare) is observable and directly links setup to payoff, making the connection a narrative subversion rather than a thematic echo.
